title: A VOLUNTARY PAYMENT WHERE RATES USED TO BE
summary: Bevan asked how the Forest Products Commission will pay rates-equivalent amounts to local governments for FPC-owned plantation land going forward, as the commission buys up more land under the softwood program.
ask: Bevan asked how the Forest Products Commission will pay the rates-equivalent amount to local governments for FPC-owned land and plantations going forward.
answer: The minister said the FPC pays two ways: for land registered to DBCA it pays rates to DBCA, and for FPC-registered land it pays a rates-equivalent to the Department of Treasury, which will rise over the forward estimates as land is acquired. She said the FPC had voluntarily paid a one-off amount for 2025–26 and 2026–27 while working with shires on partnerships and other arrangements.
finding: As the FPC buys up plantation land, the payment to affected shires is currently a voluntary one-off rather than a settled, ongoing rates-equivalent arrangement.

title: SOFTWOOD EXPANSION UNDER HALFWAY BY 2026
summary: Bevan checked progress on the $350 million Softwood Plantation Investment Program, asking how many hectares had been secured against the 33,000-hectare target by 2031 and how many pine trees had been planted toward the 50 million-tree target.
ask: Bevan asked how many hectares of softwood plantation had been purchased under the $350 million program, whether it was on track for 33,000 hectares by 2031, and how many trees had been planted toward the 50 million-tree target.
answer: The minister said that as of this month the Forest Products Commission had secured 15,000 hectares in total, 13,000 of it freehold and share-farmland, at roughly 1,300 trees per hectare, and took the total tree count on notice.
finding: Five years from its 2031 target, the softwood plantation program has secured 15,000 of the 33,000 hectares it is meant to deliver, under halfway there.

title: THE SANDALWOOD CLIFF, AND NO PLAN FOR THE PULLERS
summary: As shadow Minister for Forestry, Bevan pressed on the wild sandalwood order-in-council that expires on 31 December 2026, asking when the replacement will be finalised, what consultation has occurred with pullers, processors, businesses and traditional owners, and what support is being considered for affected communities if harvest volumes are cut.
ask: Bevan asked when the new sandalwood order would be finalised, what consultation had occurred with pullers, processors, businesses and traditional owners, and what support is being considered for affected communities and businesses if harvest volumes are reduced.
answer: The minister said the current order expires on 31 December 2026 and all Forest Products Commission sandalwood contracts terminate by the end of 2026, that the replacement is under review by a DBCA working group with an independent expert panel, that consultation would occur as required and was a matter for DBCA, and that any support for affected communities is unknowable until the review concludes. She said sandalwood is marginal to the FPC, less than 10 per cent of the business.
finding: Sandalwood harvesting contracts end in 2026, but no transition support for affected pullers and businesses has been decided, and consultation has been deferred to a separate department's review.

title: ELDER-ABUSE FUNDING, FLAT AND UNCERTAIN
summary: Bevan examined funding for elder-abuse prevention and elder rights, asking why the Advocare elder-abuse prevention service funding is ending, how demand for Elder Rights WA has changed, and whether current funding is enough to meet rising demand.
ask: Bevan asked why funding for the Advocare elder-abuse prevention service is coming to an end, how many people accessed Elder Rights WA last year compared with five years ago, and whether current funding meets rising demand from elder abuse, financial coercion and housing insecurity.
answer: The minister said the Advocare service is fixed-term funded to 2027–28 and that ongoing funding is a consideration for future budgets; that Elder Rights WA delivered 2,454 services to 411 clients in 2024–25 but that no historical demand data was available to compare; and that its core funding has not increased beyond a non-ongoing operational top-up.
finding: Elder-abuse prevention and elder-rights services are funded flat or fixed-term, with no committed continuation and no demand data to show whether funding is keeping pace.

title: FOUR NEW AGED-CARE ROLES, ALL IN THE CITY
summary: Bevan asked about the four new aged-care Hospital in the Home coordinators funded under the winter strategy, including the budget and the locations they would serve.
ask: Bevan asked where the four new aged-care Hospital in the Home coordinators would be based and which locations they would service.
answer: The minister said the program is funded at $1.6 million for four coordinators under the 2026 winter strategy, that they would be attached to each health service provider, and that they would all be metropolitan based.
finding: All four new aged-care Hospital in the Home coordinators will be metropolitan based, with none in the regions.

title: A $100M SCHEME THAT RUNS ONCE
summary: Bevan raised feedback to his office that the $100 million aged-care low-interest loan scheme may not be financially sustainable for smaller communities, asked how many applications had been received and whether there would be a second round, and whether more would follow given the scale of the bed shortfall.
ask: Bevan asked whether the $100 million aged-care loan scheme would run a second round or exhaust the funding in one round, and whether the government would commit more, noting feedback that the model may not be sustainable for smaller communities.
answer: The minister said applications were still being evaluated, that the scheme is expected to exhaust the full $100 million in one round with no second round, and that the state's own analysis now puts the shortfall at between 1,600 and 2,700 additional beds by 2029–30, requiring a $1 billion to $1.8 billion market investment, with no new state commitment beyond the existing scheme.
finding: The $100 million loan scheme is expected to run once and exhaust, against a revised shortfall the state now puts at up to 2,700 beds and $1.8 billion, with no further state funding committed.

title: 366 WAITING IN HOSPITAL, NO REGIONAL COUNT
summary: As shadow Minister for Aged Care, Bevan asked for a regional breakdown of older patients stuck in hospital waiting for residential aged care. The minister gave a current statewide figure but said no regional breakdown is being compiled.
ask: Bevan asked for a breakdown of the proportion of delayed-discharge patients waiting in hospital for residential aged care who come from regional areas.
answer: The minister said that as of the previous day, 366 older adults over 65 were in WA hospitals waiting for residential aged care, but that she did not have a regional breakdown and was not sure one was being compiled, adding that the situation in some regions is less acute because the WA Country Health Service provides aged care there.
finding: The state could give a statewide count of older people stuck in hospital but could not say how many come from the regions, because that breakdown is not being compiled.

title: ONLY 55% OF PRIORITY BURNS DONE
summary: Bevan questioned why only 55 per cent of planned priority-one prescribed burns were achieved, asking whether the shortfall was a staffing or funding problem given how important prescribed burning is to keeping South West communities safe.
ask: Bevan asked why only 55 per cent of planned priority-one prescribed burns were achieved, and whether the shortfall was due to staffing, funding or weather, and whether more funding would help.
answer: The parliamentary secretary and officials attributed the result to weather and environmental conditions, saying 2024–25 was drier with fewer safe burning windows, that conditions had improved this season with about 173,000 hectares burnt against 111,000 hectares at the same point a year earlier, and that with $30 million and 34 additional staff there were no resourcing issues affecting priority-one burns.
finding: The department attributed the 55 per cent priority-one burn result to weather rather than funding, saying recent investment means resourcing is not the constraint.

title: ONE TREE, OR 114? THE TINGLE BURN
summary: As shadow Minister for Forestry, Bevan raised the prescribed burn that destroyed threatened tingle trees in Walpole–Nornalup National Park in late 2025, an event that drew international attention, and asked how the department came to state publicly that one large tree had been lost when the true figure was far higher.
ask: Bevan asked how DBCA came to state publicly that one large tree was destroyed in the Walpole–Nornalup prescribed burn when, he said, 114 trees were lost, including 95 red tingles.
answer: After a point of order that the question went to a past media statement rather than a budget line item, the parliamentary secretary declined to answer, asking the member to tie the question to a specific line item, and the Chair suggested it might be a matter for a different forum.
finding: The discrepancy between the department's public statement of one tree and the figure of 114 trees, including 95 red tingles, that Bevan put to the committee was not explained.

title: NO AUDITED STANDARD FOR A SUCCESSFUL OFFSET
summary: In the Water and Environmental Regulation estimates, Bevan tested the integrity of the state's vegetation offsets, asking whether there is a published, independently audited standard for what counts as a successful offset and what happens, and who pays, when an offset fails.
ask: Bevan asked whether the department has a published, independently audited standard for a successful vegetation offset, and where the funded remediation sits if an offset fails through drought, pest or fire.
answer: Officials said offsets vary by project and that WA's offset policy reform is still underway, delayed by the Commonwealth's own offset standards which had only just been released, so the state policy is not yet formalised. On failure, the director general said remediation depends on the offset type and is generally tied to a predetermined period, with replanting usually undertaken if a fire passes through a replanted area within that period.
finding: WA has no formalised, independently audited standard for a successful vegetation offset, and remediation when one fails is handled case by case within a set period rather than through a dedicated funded mechanism.

title: THE HOMELESSNESS DATA THAT DOESN'T SEE THE REGIONS
summary: In the Community Services estimates, Bevan tested how youth homelessness investment is targeted, asking whether the data is broken down between metropolitan and regional areas and whether the state knows how many young people are homeless in regional WA.
ask: Bevan asked whether the homelessness by-name list data is broken down between metropolitan and regional areas, and whether the state has a reliable figure for youth homelessness in regional WA.
answer: The parliamentary secretary said the by-name list does not capture the whole state, covering only Perth city, Bunbury, Geraldton, Mandurah and Rockingham, and that there is no reliable statewide figure for youth homelessness because the data is hard to capture and the census count comes only every five years.
finding: The data used to direct homelessness investment does not cover most of regional WA, so communities like those in Warren–Blackwood are largely invisible in it.

title: A SUCCESS RATE THAT MAY NOT COUNT DEATHS
summary: Examining the Department of Communities' family and domestic violence re-response effectiveness indicator, which counts clients who did not require another FDV response within 12 months, Bevan questioned whether a victim who was killed or seriously harmed before re-engaging would appear in the measure at all.
ask: Bevan asked whether a client who died as a result of family and domestic violence after contact with the department's FDV service would show up anywhere in the re-response indicator, and where such a death would be recorded.
answer: The minister said clients who remained in an open case for 12 months are excluded, that a death may or may not show up, and that it would not appear at all if it was not a child-protection-related service in the first place.
finding: On the department's own answer, a victim who dies after contact with the service may not be captured in the indicator used to measure that service's success. Support is available: 1800RESPECT on 1800 737 732, or 000 in an emergency.

title: WHAT DOES IT TAKE TO JUSTIFY A REFUGE?
summary: In the Prevention of Family and Domestic Violence estimates, Bevan pressed the minister on how refuge locations are chosen, what data justifies building one, and why Warren–Blackwood is not among the five new refuges, asking directly what dataset he should look at and what the department needs from his community to make the case.
ask: Bevan asked why Warren–Blackwood is not among the five new refuges, what metrics or dataset the department needs to justify building one, and what his community needs to provide to make the case.
answer: The minister said it was not a case of Warren–Blackwood missing out, that the electorate is serviced by a number of FDV providers, and that the South West, the Kimberley and Perth's northern suburbs are assessed as the three highest-need regions using Australian Bureau of Statistics, Australian Institute of Health and Welfare, police and service-provider data. She did not commit to a Warren–Blackwood refuge.
finding: The minister declined to commit to a Warren–Blackwood refuge, pointing to statewide need data and existing service providers. Support is available: 1800RESPECT on 1800 737 732, or 000 in an emergency.

title: ONE TAFE PROJECT NAMED FOR THE WHOLE SOUTH WEST
summary: In the Training and Workforce Development estimates, Bevan worked through the South Regional TAFE asset program and established that Collie is the only South West TAFE infrastructure project named in the budget, then asked what funded pathway exists for the rest.
ask: Bevan worked through the South Regional TAFE asset program, establishing that Collie is the only South West TAFE project named in the budget, and asked what funded infrastructure pathway exists for the rest of South Regional TAFE, naming Bunbury, Margaret River, Manjimup, Albany, Esperance and Denmark as absent.
answer: The minister confirmed Collie is the only named South Regional TAFE project and described the rest as election commitments, while the director general pointed to strategic asset planning, a remedial works budget and the TAFE modern equipment program rather than a named, funded project for the other South West campuses.
finding: Beyond Collie, no South West TAFE campus has a named, funded infrastructure project in the budget.

title: THE WORK IS DONE; WE'RE WAITING ON THE GOVERNMENT
summary: Bevan said the case for a Warren–Blackwood refuge is no longer hypothetical: his office has a costed model, has identified delivery partners and consulted community organisations across the electorate, the WA Police Union has formally written in support, and the community is fully behind it. He said that after the minister came to the electorate and pointed to a metropolitan local government facility as a locally funded model, his office visited and found it operates from Department of Communities property and receives state funding, which shows such facilities depend on state support. He noted the Wimmera region in Victoria and North West Tasmania built local responses rather than telling women to travel, and quoted the Premier's comment on ABC Great Southern radio that this is not a party-political issue, agreeing and saying the policy work the Premier said they were waiting on is done and on the table.
quote: The work is done, the community is ready and we are waiting on the Labor government.
outcome: Bevan's second grievance on the absence of a family and domestic violence refuge in Warren–Blackwood, taken by the Minister for Aged Care and Seniors; Women in the absence of the Prevention of Family and Domestic Violence Minister. Support is available: 1800RESPECT on 1800 737 732, or 000 in an emergency.

title: $564 MILLION STATEWIDE, NOT ONE CENT HERE
summary: In his second grievance on the absence of a family and domestic violence refuge in Warren–Blackwood, Bevan said he was raising it again because nothing on the ground had changed in the 160 days since his first. He said that since 2017 the government has committed more than $564 million to family and domestic violence services statewide, including a December package for refuges in Albany, Broome and Midland, but not one cent has delivered a refuge or a single crisis bed in his electorate. He said the Bunbury refuge turned away 817 women and children last year and Busselton turned away 286 inquiries in six months, and that across the state an estimated 15,000 family violence incidents were recorded in those 160 days, one every 15 minutes, while a woman fleeing Manjimup faces a return trip to Bunbury that takes almost a whole police shift.
quote: A woman in danger should not have to leave her community to be safe.
outcome: Bevan's second grievance on the absence of a family and domestic violence refuge in Warren–Blackwood, taken by the Minister for Aged Care and Seniors; Women in the absence of the Prevention of Family and Domestic Violence Minister. Support is available: 1800RESPECT on 1800 737 732, or 000 in an emergency.

title: DANGEROUS ROADS, NONE OF THEM NAMED
summary: Bevan said the budget commits $113.5 million to Main Roads statewide maintenance but names none of the roads his communities drive every day. He pointed to the South Western Highway between Manjimup and Walpole, repeatedly identified as one of WA's most dangerous, with narrow lanes, soft shoulders and limited overtaking; the Manjimup to Bridgetown freight and tourist corridor with nowhere to overtake safely; and the Bussell Highway through Cowaramup with inadequate pedestrian infrastructure. He also raised Margaret River Independent School, which sits on a road with a 110 kilometre per hour limit and no pull-off lane, and called on the Ministers for Education and Transport to treat it as a safety priority rather than a planning matter.
quote: Margaret River Independent School sits on a road with an 110 kilometre per hour speed limit. There is no pull-off lane. It is an accident waiting to happen.
outcome: Part of Bevan's second reply to the state budget, measured against a single standard he set for the government: name the town, fund it with a line item, and set a date to deliver it.

title: SILENCE ON THE AGED-CARE LOANS SINCE FEBRUARY
summary: As shadow Minister for Aged Care, Bevan examined the Treasurer's $104 million interest-free loan package for aged care and said the Peel and Rockingham–Kwinana health campuses received dedicated aged-care capital but he could find no equivalent for the South West's small regional providers. He said more than 300 patients across regional WA are waiting for aged-care placements, a figure from the Treasurer's own speech, and that the gap between Commonwealth funding and the real cost of care is around $119 per resident per day, over $4 million a year for a 100-bed facility. He said providers in and near his electorate lodged applications for the loan facility in February, the program was oversubscribed, but there had been no communication, timeline or outcome since, and that providers cannot plan, sign contracts or commit to staff without certainty.
quote: The silence from the government since February is not acceptable.
outcome: Part of Bevan's second reply to the state budget, measured against a single standard he set for the government: name the town, fund it with a line item, and set a date to deliver it.

title: NAME THE TOWN, FUND IT, DELIVER IT
summary: In his second budget reply, Bevan said he read both volumes of budget paper No 2 and the rest looking not for political ammunition but for the names of his towns, and found Warren–Blackwood's major communities almost invisible. He said Denmark, Margaret River, Bridgetown, Boyup Brook, Nannup, Pemberton and Northcliffe received no meaningful mention, Manjimup appeared four times including once for an education office maintenance line, and only Cowaramup received a real commitment, a school upgrade promised before the election. He set a three-word standard for the government, named, funded and delivered, meaning the town named in the budget papers, a dollar figure against a specific project, and a delivery date people can hold the government to, and said the budget failed that test for his electorate in nearly every area that matters.
quote: I was not looking for political ammunition; I was looking for the names of our towns.
outcome: Part of Bevan's second reply to the state budget, measured against a single standard he set for the government: name the town, fund it with a line item, and set a date to deliver it.

title: A $17,000 BILL TO CLOSE A ROAD FOR A FESTIVAL
summary: Bevan said an October 2024 Main Roads change set a mandatory minimum rate of $37.24 an hour for traffic controllers on state roads, with the right aim of worker safety but applied as a metropolitan standard rolled out unchanged across the state. He said an entry-level traffic controller now attracts a base wage of around $73,000 to $78,000, rising above $116,000 over a 50-hour week, costs that may suit a busy metropolitan road but not a small shire doing routine maintenance. He said the Manjimup Cherry Festival faces a $17,000 traffic management bill to close roads for one day, putting the festival at risk, and that when the Shire of Manjimup raised it, the Transport minister said there were no plans to change the policy or introduce subsidies. He called for a genuine review of how the requirements apply in regional settings.
quote: A scheme built around metropolitan conditions should not be applied unchanged to regional roads.
outcome: Part of Bevan's contribution supporting the motion recognising local governments and the pressure the state's decisions place on small regional shires.

title: A DONATION IS NOT A SOLUTION
summary: Bevan explained how the Forest Products Commission rates shortfall came about: pine plantations once sat on DBCA-owned land that the FPC leased and paid rates on, but as part of the native forestry closure the state gave the FPC $350 million to buy about 33,000 hectares outright, and because the Forest Products Act exempts land it owns from rates, the payments to local governments disappeared, a foreseeable consequence with no mechanism put in place. He said the Warren–Blackwood shortfall is around $325,000 this financial year, with Boyup Brook alone exposed to more than $135,000, around 3 per cent of its rate revenue, and that the shire would have needed to lift rates from 7 to about 10 per cent had it known. He acknowledged the government made a one-off donation payment to affected shires but said budgeting needs certainty, not goodwill, and that the gap will keep widening as the FPC acquires more land.
quote: A donation is not a solution. It is a one-off.
outcome: Part of Bevan's contribution supporting the motion recognising local governments and the pressure the state's decisions place on small regional shires.

title: WE GENERATE THE WEALTH, THEN GO CAP IN HAND
summary: In his reply supporting the budget amendment, Bevan said WA's wealth comes from the regions, with $220 billion in resources sales and more than $10.8 billion in mining royalties last year, and that the lithium powering the global electric vehicle transition comes from Greenbushes in Warren–Blackwood. He said Talison's Greenbushes operation, the largest hard rock lithium mine in the world, produced nearly 1.5 million tonnes of concentrate and contributed more than $853 million to governments in 2024, yet the town's primary school made the front page over long-overdue maintenance. He said Talison is not a regional ATM and should not be expected to fund basic community services when the government falls short, and that royalties flow from Greenbushes, the Goldfields and the Pilbara to Perth and should be invested back to make regional communities liveable.
quote: Talison should not be the regional ATM in the government's absence.
outcome: Part of Bevan's reply to the state budget, supporting the amendment moved by the member for Cottesloe and arguing that the regions generate the state's wealth but are made to go cap in hand for the basics.

title: 26 THREATENED SPECIES, NO FULL ASSESSMENT
summary: Bevan rose on behalf of Augusta community members concerned about the approval process for the proposed Scott River wind farm, saying this was not about being for or against the project, since WA needs reliable energy and renewables are part of that, but about whether the process was done right. He said the Scott River region sits within one of only 35 globally recognised biodiversity hotspots, with 26 threatened fauna species in a catchment that drains to the Blackwood River and Flinders Bay, and that the nearby Beenup mineral sands mine opened in 1997 and later closed over environmental, technical and community concerns. He said that despite documented acid sulphate soils and the threatened species, the Environmental Protection Authority determined the proposal did not warrant a full formal assessment, and that the community is asking for transparent and genuine engagement, not to be labelled as NIMBYs.
quote: If that combination does not meet the threshold, the community is entitled to ask what does.
outcome: Delivered as a member's statement on behalf of Augusta community members questioning the approval process for the proposed Scott River wind farm.

title: A FEW TREES, A FLAME-ZONE RATING, A LOST BUILD
summary: Bevan said even where people are ready to build, the planning system is making it harder, particularly through inconsistent bushfire attack level ratings. He cited a fully serviced R20 property in Bridgetown given a BAL Flame Zone rating because a small number of trees were labelled a forest, which he said prevented subdivision and even a basic boundary realignment and effectively shut down a straightforward development, while genuinely high-risk areas like Quinninup were treated differently. He said the cost of complying with BAL requirements is driving people to walk away from builds, that vegetation definitions appear to be applied too broadly with little transparency, and that a lack of enabling infrastructure such as water, power and servicing in towns like Northcliffe, Nannup and Pemberton is compounding the problem.
quote: This is not a system that supports housing delivery; it is a system that slows it down or stops it altogether.
outcome: Part of Bevan's contribution supporting the motion condemning the Cook Labor government for failing its commitment that every Western Australian has a home, measured against the government's own criteria.

title: EVERY WESTERN AUSTRALIAN, EXCEPT THIS ONE
summary: Bevan supported a motion condemning the government for failing its own commitment, in its own words, that every Western Australian has a home. He said the week's $692 million regional housing package named seven cities but nothing for Warren–Blackwood, even though the electorate meets every one of the government's criteria: a rental vacancy rate below 1 per cent, against around 3 per cent in Broome which was funded, population growth of more than 8,400 since 2016, and a social housing rate around half the regional average. He said Margaret River, Bridgetown and Denmark are at breaking point, and that Government Regional Officers' Housing has seen a net increase of just 78 homes across the entire state in a decade.
quote: If the language means something, funding must follow. If it does not follow, the language means nothing.
outcome: Part of Bevan's contribution supporting the motion condemning the Cook Labor government for failing its commitment that every Western Australian has a home, measured against the government's own criteria.

title: OVER $40 MILLION FOR ONE METRO REFUGE, NOTHING HERE
summary: Bevan asked about the $18.1 million in the budget to redevelop a refuge in the City of Stirling, adding 15 units, and whether it was on top of the $22.6 million already committed to the Stirling Women's Centre redevelopment, bringing investment in one metropolitan location to over $40 million. He asked why his communities in Warren–Blackwood continue to miss out on equivalent family and domestic violence funding, and in a supplementary asked the minister to commit to fund and develop a Warren–Blackwood refuge as a matter of urgency.
quote: Will the minister commit to fund and develop a much-needed FDV refuge in the Warren–Blackwood region as a matter of urgency?
outcome: The minister, Jessica Stojkovski, pointed to 57 family and domestic violence units in the pipeline for remote and regional WA, the majority regional, and to more than $700 million invested since 2017 and 108 new units delivered since then, but did not commit to a refuge in Warren–Blackwood, saying it was her job to assess where the biggest need was and that her department would keep working with the member. Support is available: 1800RESPECT on 1800 737 732, or 000 in an emergency.

title: STRONGER PENALTIES, BUT KEEP THE RIGHT TO RECOURSE
summary: Bevan supported the bill lifting penalties for offences under the act, from $10,000 to $50,000 for individuals and to $250,000 for corporations, saying the old penalties were set in a different era and that during a fuel emergency a $10,000 fine is a rounding error for some corporate players, while 78 infringement notices were issued in the recent crisis. He flagged two concerns: clause 9 removes the existing right to have a compensation matter referred to arbitration before a judge, making the minister's decision final, which he said could leave regional businesses that suffered real losses without independent recourse; and the bill's two Henry VIII clauses, which allow regulations to be made without returning to Parliament. He sought assurance the compensation framework would remain accessible to regional businesses.
quote: A $250,000 penalty focuses minds and changes behaviour.
outcome: A contribution to the second reading debate on the Fuel, Energy and Power Resources Amendment Bill 2026, supporting stronger penalties and fuel-supply monitoring while pressing for regional transparency and safeguards.

title: REGIONAL WA WAS FLYING BLIND ON FUEL PRICES
summary: Speaking on the Fuel, Energy and Power Resources Amendment Bill 2026, Bevan said FuelWatch has been a metropolitan-centric scheme that for years covered all metropolitan retail outlets but only around 80 per cent of regional sites, leaving about 20 per cent of regional retailers off the radar so motorists in towns across the regions had no way to compare prices and simply paid what was at the bowser. He credited the change requiring every WA retailer to report from 1 May 2026, bringing in about 203 additional retailers and, for the first time, requiring fuel supply outages to be reported, but said it was welcome and long overdue, and that the 2026 fuel crisis had exposed how fragile regional supply chains and their monitoring had been.
quote: Perth motorists have had price transparency for years, whereas regional motorists have been flying blind.
outcome: A contribution to the second reading debate on the Fuel, Energy and Power Resources Amendment Bill 2026, supporting stronger penalties and fuel-supply monitoring while pressing for regional transparency and safeguards.

title: THE NUMBERS NO LONGER ADD UP FOR FARMERS
summary: Bevan said farmers are facing rising input costs across the board, with urea fertiliser surging from about $900 to $1,800 a tonne, if it can be obtained at all, and freight companies applying fuel levies of up to 60 to 67 per cent that growers cannot pass on because they are price takers. He said some fertiliser companies are no longer taking new or even existing clients, and the horticultural sector faces huge uncertainty about what it can plant. He gave the example of transporting a crate of 12 cauliflowers from the Manjimup region to Perth, once about $3 and now 67 per cent more on top of higher costs, and said growers are telling him they will not plant horticultural crops next year unless this is addressed.
quote: There comes a point at which the numbers simply do not add up for the farmer to continue to grow their crops.
outcome: Part of Bevan's contribution supporting the fuel-security motion, walking through how fuel and input costs are hitting regional households, businesses, farmers and aged-care providers.

title: AGED CARE DELIVERED ON THE ROAD
summary: As shadow Minister for Aged Care, Bevan said regional aged care is delivered on the road, with carers travelling long distances between clients, often across several towns in a day, while their travel reimbursements are fixed, capped or based on the ATO cents-per-kilometre rate that does not reflect current fuel prices, vehicle wear on regional roads or the distances involved. He said fuel instability is turning an existing pressure into a breaking point, forcing providers to absorb rising costs, reduce services or stretch a thin workforce, which means fewer visits, longer waits and, in some cases, providers deciding whether they can keep servicing certain areas at all.
quote: Aged care in regional WA is delivered on the road.
outcome: Part of Bevan's contribution supporting the fuel-security motion, walking through how fuel and input costs are hitting regional households, businesses, farmers and aged-care providers.

title: FUEL HITS THE REGIONS HARDEST
summary: Bevan said fuel security is a direct and immediate threat to food security, and that while it is a whole-of-state issue, it is not felt equally. He said in the city fuel is often a background cost, but in the regions it is a daily constraint, with people travelling two to four hours for a doctor, hundreds of kilometres a week for school, and no public transport to fall back on, while regional diesel has risen from about $1.70 to more than $3.45 a litre. He said the Denmark Chamber of Commerce reports businesses across tourism, construction, freight, health and local services facing cancelled bookings, rising costs, paused hiring and even closure, with one business losing more than $44,000 in revenue in under a month. He added that regional flights are a lifeline for patients, families and volunteers, and that the Patient Assisted Travel Scheme and support for pensioners on fixed incomes do not reflect the real distances and fuel costs.
quote: In the city, fuel is often a background cost. In the regions, it is a daily constraint.
outcome: Part of Bevan's contribution supporting the fuel-security motion, walking through how fuel and input costs are hitting regional households, businesses, farmers and aged-care providers.

title: NO DIESEL FOR THE HARVEST
summary: Bevan said the government has no regional food security plan and no framework for prioritising fuel, and that the gap is now showing in diesel shortages that threaten food production, since diesel runs irrigation, harvesting and transport. He said growers face losing crops that took months to produce, with potatoes taking up to 22 weeks and orchardists having one harvest a year and no second chance, while fuel distributors that supply farmers receive only a fraction of what they need. He contrasted this with Singapore, which he said is investing around $70 million to secure its food supply ahead of time, and argued WA is exporting the solution and importing the problem, pointing to a Perth company exporting biomass to Singapore for renewable fuel while WA does not build a biodiesel industry from its own feedstock and land.
quote: A system that cannot guarantee fuel at harvest time is a system that is failing its most basic responsibility.
outcome: Part of Bevan's contribution supporting the motion condemning the Cook Labor government over its performance in the regions, walking through a pattern across forestry, fuel and food production.

title: THE STATE TAKES THE REVENUE, THE REGION PAYS
summary: Bevan said the decision to redirect Forest Products Commission rates from local governments to Treasury is a direct cost shift onto regional communities, leaving Warren–Blackwood shires about $325,000 short this financial year, with the Shire of Boyup Brook alone losing more than $135,000, around 3 per cent of its rate revenue. He said the rules changed after the shire's budget was locked in, with no warning or consultation, forcing it to wind back investment in roads, bridges and community infrastructure, with more than $170,000 in local spending at risk including events, grants and support for volunteers and St John Ambulance. He added that changes to hauling rules have stripped councils of the ability to make those who damage local roads pay, pointing to a single Boyup Brook haulage route with repair costs of about $323,000 falling on the shire, even though the plantation generating the traffic is not in that shire.
quote: The state takes the revenue, the state sets the rules, but the region pays the bills.
outcome: Part of Bevan's contribution supporting the motion condemning the Cook Labor government over its performance in the regions, walking through a pattern across forestry, fuel and food production.

title: HOME CARE UP 52% FOR ONE FAMILY
summary: Bevan said home care is becoming less available and less affordable, with WA well below the national average for in-home support, 4.7 recipients per 1,000 people over 65 against 7.7 nationally, and fewer high-level packages. He gave a constituent case handled by his office in which the effective cost of the same home-care package rose from about $154 an hour, roughly $1,613 a week, to about $234 an hour, around $2,456 a week, under the new pricing structure, an increase of about $843 a week or $43,800 a year, around 52 per cent. He said he was not suggesting providers were acting improperly, as many were adjusting to the structural design of the reforms, but that when home care becomes more expensive, seniors cut back or delay support, preventive care is missed and small problems become crises that end in hospital.
quote: When home care provision is more expensive, many seniors reduce their use of services, change provider or delay accessing support altogether.
outcome: Part of Bevan's contribution supporting the matter of public interest motion on whether Western Australians can have confidence the health system will cope, particularly heading into winter. He used it to put emergency department, ambulance and aged-care pressures on the parliamentary record.

title: PAYING HOSPITAL PRICES FOR AGED CARE
summary: Bevan said the biggest pressure on hospital capacity sits outside the hospital, in aged care. He said WA has fewer residential aged-care places than the national average, 59.1 per 1,000 older people against 65.4 nationally, and higher occupancy at 92 per cent, so older patients who are medically ready for discharge often have nowhere to go. About 200 hospital beds on any given day are occupied by such patients, which doctors describe as a small hospital's worth of people who no longer need hospital care, and the Auditor General found patients waiting for aged-care or disability services spent around 40,000 extra days in hospital, more than 80 days each. He said a hospital bed costs about $2,300 a day against about $350 in aged care, so the state spends roughly $173 million a year delivering in hospitals what aged care could provide for about $26 million, a gap of around $147 million.
quote: Western Australia is paying hospital prices to deliver aged-care services simply because the right care is not available at the right time.
outcome: Part of Bevan's contribution supporting the matter of public interest motion on whether Western Australians can have confidence the health system will cope, particularly heading into winter. He used it to put emergency department, ambulance and aged-care pressures on the parliamentary record.

title: THE LONGEST ED WAITS IN THE COUNTRY
summary: Speaking to a matter of public interest motion on whether the health system can cope, Bevan said recent national data shows Western Australia now has the longest emergency department wait times in the country, with 90 per cent of patients waiting up to 229 minutes to be seen, almost double the national figure. He said ambulance ramping in September last year reached 7,257 hours, the highest ever recorded, and that every ramped hour is a crew that cannot answer the next call. He said healthcare workers are not the problem but the reason the system keeps functioning, and that with winter's predictable surge in respiratory illness approaching, the government has not produced a comprehensive plan to show the system is prepared.
quote: When ambulances are ramped outside hospitals, people do not see a system performing strongly; they see a system under pressure.
outcome: Part of Bevan's contribution supporting the matter of public interest motion on whether Western Australians can have confidence the health system will cope, particularly heading into winter. He used it to put emergency department, ambulance and aged-care pressures on the parliamentary record.

title: 368 ELDERLY WAITING IN HOSPITAL BEDS
summary: Twelve months on from the March election, Bevan asked the Minister for Aged Care for the exact number of patients who are medically ready for discharge but waiting in hospital for a residential aged-care placement, and what the state is doing to ease the pressure on hospitals. In a supplementary he asked when she had last spoken to her federal counterpart to demand WA's fair share ahead of winter.
quote: What exact number of medically ready for discharge patients are currently waiting in hospital for residential aged-care placement?
outcome: The Minister for Aged Care, Simone McGurk, said that as of 8 March, 368 people were waiting in hospital for residential aged care or a home-care package. She said federal capital funding would deliver about 50 beds in Perth against a forecast need of 2,800 by 2030–31, pointed to the state's $100 million low-interest loan scheme then under evaluation and the Time to Think program, which she said had discharged 332 long-stay patients and freed more than 9,000 hospital bed days, and said she had last raised WA's share with her federal counterpart the previous Thursday.

title: THE PEOPLE WHO HOLD OUR TOWNS TOGETHER
summary: In a member's statement, Bevan recognised the Citizen of the Year and community award recipients across Warren–Blackwood. He named, among others, Joanna Cleverly and Jenny Forbes in Nannup for hospital support and St John ambulance service; Dr Sarah Youngson and the Rotary Club of Bridgetown for regional health care and youth wellbeing; Mary-Anne Inglis, David Cowan and David Fortune in Boyup Brook for sport, volunteerism and service; Lionel Lee and Lee Shuard in Manjimup for ambulance, youth cadets and civic leadership; the Denmark Volunteer Fire and Rescue Service, including Shaun Ossinger, Lesley Murphy and Paul Sleeman; and Anthea Redding, Izzy Forte, Brendan Jordan and the Margaret River Lions Club. He said they reflect the very best of regional Western Australia.
quote: Across our towns and districts, these individuals and organisations reflect the very best of regional Western Australia.
outcome: Delivered as a member's statement recognising the Citizen of the Year and community award recipients across Warren–Blackwood.

title: A QUIET MARINA, A DANGEROUS BOAT RAMP
summary: Bevan said the closures have left Augusta's purpose-built Royalties for Regions marina markedly quieter, with fuel turnover, bait and tackle sales, marine servicing and hospitality spending all down, while displaced activity has shifted to Windy Harbour, a small coastal settlement on the exposed Southern Ocean never designed for the traffic. He said boat ramps there are buckling, trailer parks are overflowing and operators are launching in treacherous conditions, raising real safety risk. He added that local shark fishers had supplied the Augusta Hotel with fresh, affordable fish for more than 20 years, and that with local supply stopped, product is now freighted in, for example to Walpole from Albany, pushing seafood prices up for families at a time of cost-of-living pressure.
quote: We have gone from a purpose-built marina in Augusta to people now launching in treacherous conditions.
outcome: Part of Bevan's contribution supporting the disallowance motion on the commercial fishing closures, putting the process, the regional economic impact and the safety consequences on the parliamentary record.

title: A SUSTAINABLE FISHERY, CLOSED OVER CHRISTMAS
summary: Speaking in support of a disallowance motion on the commercial fishing closures, Bevan said the issue is not whether fisheries should be sustainable, they must be, but the process. He said a report last year confirmed the commercial fishing industry was operating sustainably and that the fishers had complied with every rule, yet were given three weeks' notice over Christmas, many learning through social media rather than a departmental briefing, to unwind businesses built over generations, with boats still under finance and crews to stand down. He questioned what science had changed when bag limits had been increased only recently under the previous minister, and said the southern demersal gillnet and longline fishery has been managed under strict controls since 1989 and is regarded as one of the most sustainable shark fisheries in the world, with closures extending to capture around 40 per cent of zone 1 that lies outside the stated west coast bioregion boundary. He placed it in a pattern alongside native timber and live sheep export.
quote: These commercial fishers have done absolutely nothing wrong. They have followed every rule.
outcome: Part of Bevan's contribution supporting the disallowance motion on the commercial fishing closures, putting the process, the regional economic impact and the safety consequences on the parliamentary record.

title: A CLOSURE ORDER COULD SHUT A TOWN'S ONLY STORE
summary: Bevan raised the bill's closure-order powers, under which premises can be closed for 72 hours administratively and up to 90 days by ministerial order. He said that in a metropolitan area there may be alternatives, but in a small regional town the closure of the only general store can mean no local groceries, no fuel and no essential goods, and that the bill provides no mandatory expedited review timeframe and no compensation if a closure is later found to have been wrongly made. He said strong powers must be matched with strong safeguards, including a rapid review with written reasons and a clear line between deliberate trafficking and an isolated compliance breach.
quote: Strong powers must be matched with strong safeguards.
outcome: A contribution to the second reading debate on the Tobacco Products Control Amendment Bill 2026, supporting stronger action on illicit tobacco and vaping while seeking safeguards so the crackdown is workable and fair for small regional businesses.

title: HONEST RETAILERS CAUGHT BY RULES FOR CRIMINALS
summary: Speaking on the Tobacco Products Control Amendment Bill 2026, Bevan backed stronger action against the organised criminal trade in illicit tobacco and vapes, noting firebombings and attacks on shops, but said strong laws must also be workable and fair for regional WA. He said illegal tobacco and vape shops have set up in his electorate, with neighbouring businesses reporting constant fear for their safety, lost trade and rising insurance costs, including one that can no longer insure its front door after repeated damage costing about $5,000 each time. He warned the bill sets serious criminal consequences while leaving the prescribed possession-for-sale quantities to regulations not yet seen, and called for a due-diligence protection so an honest retailer who bought lawful stock from a licensed supplier is not treated like an organised criminal.
quote: Strong laws must also be workable laws.
outcome: A contribution to the second reading debate on the Tobacco Products Control Amendment Bill 2026, supporting stronger action on illicit tobacco and vaping while seeking safeguards so the crackdown is workable and fair for small regional businesses.

title: CONSULTATION AFTER THE DECISION IS MADE
summary: Bevan said consultation in the regions increasingly feels procedural rather than genuine. He pointed to the Scott River wind farm proposals, where he said landholders are not opposing renewable energy in principle but are questioning the scale and impact on an environmentally sensitive area, raising concerns about acid sulphate soils, hydrology and biodiversity, and feel the engagement came after key decisions were well advanced. He said the same pattern showed in the demersal fishing reforms, where operators were given limited notice and minimal transition certainty, and argued that when communities feel excluded from decisions, trust erodes, projects stall and investment hesitates.
quote: Regional Western Australia does not resist reform; it resists being overridden.
outcome: Part of Bevan's reply to the Premier's Statement, built around a single test: whether regional Western Australia is stronger or weaker than three years ago. He used the speech to put a series of regional pressures on the parliamentary record.

title: A 120-BED AGED-CARE PLAN, SHELVED
summary: Bevan said the regional aged-care crisis is being driven by funding settings that make new facilities financially unviable. He said the concessional residential support rate sits at about $46 a day while the real cost of delivering care is closer to $165, a gap of more than $118 per resident per day that no regional provider can absorb at scale. As a result, a planned 120-bed aged-care facility in Warren–Blackwood has been shelved, costing construction and ongoing care jobs and forcing seniors to relocate away from family to find a bed. He linked it to the hospital bottleneck, with around 200 older Western Australians in hospital beds because there is nowhere else to go and an estimated shortfall of about 2,800 residential aged-care beds statewide, and warned aged care is becoming a postcode lottery.
quote: We are fast heading towards aged care for the wealthy.
outcome: Part of Bevan's reply to the Premier's Statement, built around a single test: whether regional Western Australia is stronger or weaker than three years ago. He used the speech to put a series of regional pressures on the parliamentary record.

title: AIRBNB STOCK, NOWHERE FOR WORKERS TO LIVE
summary: Bevan said the housing pressure across Augusta, Margaret River and Denmark is a planning failure, not just a shortage, with water, sewerage, power, roads, schools and health capacity delivered years behind demand and costing more when they finally arrive. He said a substantial share of housing in tourism hotspots is being converted to Airbnb and short-stay rentals, which removes long-term rental stock so that teachers, nurses, police and hospitality workers cannot secure stable accommodation, and communities become seasonal rather than stable. He said this is not anti-tourism, which is vital to the regional economy, but that tourism growth without infrastructure planning creates distortion, and regional WA needs forward planning rather than reactive upgrades.
quote: Streets that once housed families become transit zones.
outcome: Part of Bevan's reply to the Premier's Statement, built around a single test: whether regional Western Australia is stronger or weaker than three years ago. He used the speech to put a series of regional pressures on the parliamentary record.

title: NO MOBILE COVERAGE WHEN IT MATTERS MOST
summary: Bevan said volunteer ambulance officers, firefighters and police in his electorate are too often sent to emergency call-outs and bushfires with little or no mobile coverage, and that communities are fundraising to buy Starlink units so their substations have digital connectivity. He said businesses are affected daily, and that in a year like 2026 this is completely unacceptable.
quote: Communities are raising money to purchase Starlink so that their substations will have digital connectivity.
outcome: Part of Bevan's reply to the Premier's Statement, built around a single test: whether regional Western Australia is stronger or weaker than three years ago. He used the speech to put a series of regional pressures on the parliamentary record.

title: A SANDALWOOD INDUSTRY FACING A CLIFF
summary: As shadow Minister for Forestry, Bevan warned the WA sandalwood industry, worth about $40 million a year and supporting regional jobs and meaningful Aboriginal economic participation, is facing a hard deadline with no clear path forward. Existing wild sandalwood contracts expire on 30 September 2026, yet there is no publicly released transition framework, no confirmed governance structure, no detailed funding and no operational clarity for what happens on 1 October. He said the WA Sandalwood Taskforce had recommended a path, including lifting licence allocations, building Aboriginal capacity and moving towards an Aboriginal-led model, and asked which agency will lead the transition, what funding is allocated, who will guide wild-harvest management and how compliance and illegal harvesting will be handled. He noted the private licence quota was lifted from 250 to 500 tonnes but fewer than 70 tonnes are being harvested, and said uncertainty freezes investment.
quote: Transition without transparency is not reform, it is risk.
outcome: Part of Bevan's reply to the Premier's Statement, built around a single test: whether regional Western Australia is stronger or weaker than three years ago. He used the speech to put a series of regional pressures on the parliamentary record.

title: WE IMPORT NEARLY HALF OUR FOOD
summary: Bevan opened his reply to the Premier's Statement with a test: is regional Western Australia stronger or weaker than three years ago? He argued regional strength is structural security, the ability to house, educate, treat and feed people locally, and that the state's productive base is narrowing. He said WA exported around $15.5 billion in agricultural, fisheries and forestry products in 2022, yet imports up to 46 per cent of the food it consumes, which he called exposure rather than sovereignty. He said forestry capacity has been reduced, demersal fishing decimated, water allocations tightened and live export left uncertain, and that when local production shrinks the state imports more, prices rise at the check-out and resilience falls.
quote: That is not sovereignty; that is exposure.
outcome: Part of Bevan's reply to the Premier's Statement, built around a single test: whether regional Western Australia is stronger or weaker than three years ago. He used the speech to put a series of regional pressures on the parliamentary record.
